content:
You are a strict mathematical proof verifier.

Evaluate the given candidate solution. Do not solve the problem from scratch and do not rewrite the proof. Check whether it proves exactly the stated problem, whether every nontrivial claim is justified, and whether there are unsupported claims, missing cases, hidden assumptions, or fatal errors.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Consider a $2025 \times 2025$ grid of unit squares. Matilda wishes to place on the grid some rectangular tiles, possibly of different sizes, such that each side of every tile lies on a grid line and every unit square is covered by at most one tile.
Determine the minimum number of tiles Matilda needs to place so that each row and each column of the grid has exactly one unit square that is not covered by any tile.

Candidate solution:
<solution>
Let \(n = 2025\).  The grid consists of \(n\) rows and \(n\) columns of unit squares.  A set of uncovered squares is given by a permutation \(\sigma\) of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\): row \(i\) has exactly one uncovered square, namely at column \(\sigma(i)\).  All other squares must be covered by rectangular tiles, each tile being an axis‑aligned rectangle that covers a contiguous block of squares.  Tiles cannot overlap, and no tile may cover an uncovered square.  We are to minimise the number of tiles.

**Lower bound.**  
For a tile \(R\) with rows \([a,b]\) and columns \([c,d]\), the condition that it contains no uncovered square means that for every row \(i\in[a,b]\) we have \(\sigma(i)\notin[c,d]\).  Consequently, if \(R\) contains any square with \(j<\sigma(i)\) (the “left part’’ of row \(i\)), then necessarily the whole column interval \([c,d]\) lies strictly to the left of \(\sigma(i)\); in fact, for all rows in the interval we must have \(d<\min_{i\in[a,b]}\sigma(i)\).  Hence such a tile can only cover squares from the left part \(L=\{(i,j):j<\sigma(i)\}\).  Similarly, a tile that covers any square from the right part \(R=\{(i,j):j>\sigma(i)\}\) must have its column interval strictly to the right of all \(\sigma(i)\) of its rows, and therefore can only cover squares from \(R\).  Thus the covering splits into two independent coverings: one for \(L\) by “left‑type’’ tiles, and one for \(R\) by “right‑type’’ tiles.

Now consider the left part \(L\).  For every row \(i\) with \(\sigma(i)>1\) the cell \((i,\sigma(i)-1)\) is the rightmost cell of \(L\) in that row; it must be covered by some left‑type tile.  Let \(T\) be such a tile that covers \((i,\sigma(i)-1)\).  Because \((i,\sigma(i)-1)\in T\), the column interval of \(T\) includes the column \(\sigma(i)-1\).  Moreover, the condition that \(T\) avoids all holes forces the minimum of \(\sigma\) over the rows of \(T\) to be exactly \(\sigma(i)\); otherwise, if some row \(k\) in \(T\) satisfied \(\sigma(k)<\sigma(i)\), then the column interval would contain the column \(\sigma(k)\) and would cover the hole \((k,\sigma(k))\), a contradiction.  Consequently every row \(k\) in \(T\) satisfies \(\sigma(k)\ge\sigma(i)\).  In particular, for any other row \(j\) with \(\sigma(j)>\sigma(i)\), the cell \((j,\sigma(j)-1)\) lies in column \(\sigma(j)-1>\sigma(i)-1\), so it cannot be covered by \(T\).  Moreover, if \(j\) were in the same tile \(T\) we would have \(\sigma(j)\ge\sigma(i)\), but then \(\sigma(j)-1\) is too large to be covered.  Hence a left‑type tile can cover at most one of the cells \((i,\sigma(i)-1)\) with \(\sigma(i)>1\).  Since \(\sigma\) is a permutation, exactly one row satisfies \(\sigma(i)=1\) (the row with the hole in column 1), so there are \(n-1\) rows with \(\sigma(i)>1\).  Therefore at least \(n-1\) left‑type tiles are required to cover \(L\).

A completely analogous argument applied to the right part \(R\) shows that at least \(n-1\) right‑type tiles are needed.  Thus any covering uses at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) tiles.

**Upper bound.**  
We exhibit a construction that uses exactly \(2n-2\) tiles.  Choose the permutation \(\sigma(i)=i\) (the main diagonal).  The uncovered squares are \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\).  For each \(i=2,\dots,n\) cover the left part of row \(i\) – the squares \((i,j)\) with \(1\le j\le i-1\) – by a single tile (a \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle).  For each \(i=1,\dots,n-1\) cover the right part of row \(i\) – the squares \((i,j)\) with \(i+1\le j\le n\) – by a single tile (a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle).  These \(2n-2\) tiles are disjoint and cover every square that is not on the diagonal.  Hence \(2n-2\) tiles suffice.

**Conclusion.**  
The minimum possible number of tiles is \(2n-2 = 2\cdot2025-2 = 4048\).

\[
\boxed{4048}
\]
</solution>

Candidate self-evaluation:
<self_evaluation>
The lower bound argument correctly splits the covering into left‑type and right‑type tiles and uses the fact that each left‑type tile can cover at most one rightmost cell of \(L\) (and similarly for \(R\)).  The reasoning that a tile covering \((i,\sigma(i)-1)\) forces the minimum of \(\sigma\) in its row interval to be \(\sigma(i)\) and that any other row in the interval must have \(\sigma(j)\ge\sigma(i)\) is rigorous.  The construction for the identity permutation is valid and gives the exact bound.  All steps are justified; no gaps remain.
</self_evaluation>

Respond in EXACTLY this format:

<evaluation>
Your assessment of the solution's correctness and rigor.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
Useful ideas or repair suggestions that could help refine the proof.
</suggestions>
<score>S</score>

where S is exactly one of 0, 0.5, or 1 (nothing else inside the score tag):
- 1: completely correct, all steps properly justified.
- 0.5: generally correct, but minor gaps or omissions remain.
- 0: fatal errors, severe omissions, or does not solve the problem.
